claims.claim: A Labuan MP's appeal against Bersatu's expulsion was dismissed at the Court of Appeal this month.
claims.source: The Edge Malaysia, 29 April 2026, 'Suhaili fails in appeal to reinstate suit over cessation of Bersatu membership', https://theedgemalaysia.com/node/801842; The Star, 29 April 2026, 'Labuan MP fails in appeal to revive lawsuit over Bersatu membership cessation'
claims.correction: The appeal was dismissed on 29 April 2026. The matter was framed publicly as cessation of Bersatu membership and a Section 18C Societies Act jurisdiction issue, not simply an appeal against expulsion.
claims.stale_check: No stale-condition issue found. The claim appears to be a date/framing error rather than a later real-world change.

claims.claim: The constitutional question in the case is unanswered.
claims.source: The Edge Malaysia 29 April 2026; The Star 29 April 2026
claims.correction: It is fair to say Article 49A(2)(c) was not substantively decided. However, public reports indicate the Court of Appeal disposed of the case on Section 18C of the Societies Act 1966 and internal political-party jurisdiction grounds.

claims.claim: Article 49A inserted by Constitution (Amendment) (No. 3) Act 2022 (Act A1664).
claims.source: Laws of Malaysia, Constitution (Amendment) (No. 3) Act 2022, Act A1663, Gazette publication date 6 September 2022
claims.correction: Replace Act A1664 with Act A1663.

claims.source: Bernama, 4 October 2022, 'Anti-party hopping law to be enforced Wednesday'

claims.claim: Subsection (2)(c) exempts expulsion from triggering loss of MP status.
claims.source: Laws of Malaysia, Act A1663, Article 49A(2)(c)

claims.correction: Should be attributed to analysis unless supported by Hansard. Suggested: 'The text prevents a party from causing an MP's seat to be vacated merely by expelling that MP.'

claims.claim: Zuraida Kamaruddin's case (Court of Appeal December 2024) was decided on contracts law, not Article 49A.
claims.source: Court of Appeal judgment text, Saifuddin Nasution Ismail v Zuraida Kamaruddin, JE51/2024, judgment dated 20 December 2024

claims.claim: The court reduced PKR's RM10 million anti-defection bond to RM100,000.
claims.stale_check: A later Federal Court appeal was pending in 2026 — fixed 16 July 2026 to hear PKR appeal to reinstate the RM10 million award.

claims.claim: The court ruled that representatives cannot contract away their constitutional right to choose a party.
claims.correction: The Court of Appeal did not make the broad constitutional holding stated here. It upheld that the breach was triggered and reduced compensation under contract-law principles, especially section 75 of the Contracts Act 1950. Replace with: 'The court held that the RM10 million sum was out of proportion and reduced compensation to RM100,000 under contract-law principles.'

claims.claim: Rafizi Ramli and Nik Nazmi (May 2026) used Article 51 to vacate their seats before leaving PKR.
claims.source: Bernama 24 May 2026 EC statement; The Edge Malaysia 17 May 2026; Malay Mail 18 May 2026

claims.correction: As of May 2026, the law had been operative for about 3 years and 7 months, not four years.

claims.claim: Zero substantive judicial interpretation of the carve-out.
claims.source: No primary source found certifying complete absence of judgments.
claims.correction: Use cautious formulation: 'No public reported judgment was found in this review that decides Article 49A(2)(c) on its merits.'

claims.claim: Parties and MPs navigate around the carve-out, not through it.
claims.correction: Safer wording: 'The cited episodes reached the legal system through other routes: contract law, Societies Act jurisdiction, and Article 51 resignation. None produced a merits ruling on Article 49A(2)(c).'

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Federal Constitution Article 49A; Constitution (Amendment) (No. 3) Act 2022 (Act A1664)
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The cited Act number is wrong. The Constitution (Amendment) (No. 3) Act 2022 is Act A1663, not Act A1664.
As of May 2026, the law had been operative for about 3 years and 7 months from 5 October 2022. Calling it four years is premature unless published on or after 5 October 2026.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: Bernama 4 October 2022; Laws of Malaysia Act A1663

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: A Labuan MP's appeal against Bersatu's expulsion was dismissed at the Court of Appeal this month.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The dismissal occurred on 29 April 2026 and the case was publicly framed as cessation of Bersatu membership and a Section 18C Societies Act jurisdiction issue. The word 'expulsion' is disputed.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: The Edge Malaysia 29 April 2026; The Star 29 April 2026

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: No reported judgment interprets it on its merits.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: Absence claim. No primary source can certify complete absence across all Malaysian judgment databases and unreported written grounds.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: Needs documented search of official judiciary databases.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: The court reduced PKR's RM10 million anti-defection bond to RM100,000, ruling that representatives cannot contract away their constitutional right to choose a party.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The reduction is true, but the stated constitutional ruling is not supported by the Court of Appeal judgment. The judgment turned on contract-law compensation and proportionality under section 75 of the Contracts Act 1950 while still finding breach.
The official record supports that the vacancies were treated under Article 51. It does not conclusively establish that Article 49A could not be argued on contested facts.

omitted_facts: The Constitution (Amendment) (No. 3) Act 2022 is Act A1663, not Act A1664.
Act A1663 also deleted Article 48(6), the former five-year disqualification for MPs who voluntarily resigned, which is important context for the Article 51 resignation route.

The Suhaili Court of Appeal panel was led by Justice Azizul Azmi Adnan, with Justices Ong Chee Kwan and Amarjeet Singh Serjit Singh. The public reports state the dismissal was based on Section 18C of the Societies Act 1966 and internal political-party jurisdiction.
The Zuraida Court of Appeal judgment did not merely reduce the sum; it upheld that the breach was triggered and then reduced compensation to RM100,000 under contract-law principles.
The Zuraida matter was not final as of April 2026. Federal Court reportedly fixed 16 July 2026 to hear PKR appeal to reinstate the RM10 million award.

For Rafizi and Nik Nazmi, the Election Commission stated there would be no by-elections because the vacancies occurred within two years of the scheduled dissolution date and did not affect the government's majority, under Article 54(1).
The analysis does not include Hansard or parliamentary debate evidence for the asserted voter-protective intent of Article 49A(2)(c).
source_assessment: The analysis has a partially adequate legal-source base but relies too heavily on internal T4A materials, commentary sources, and news reports for several key claims. The constitutional text is available and strong, but the Act number is wrong. The Zuraida claim is verifiable through judgment text, but the analysis mischaracterises the holding. The Suhaili and Rafizi-Nik Nazmi sections need stronger primary records.
overall_assessment: The core thesis that Article 49A(2)(c) remains substantively unsettled is plausible, and several underlying facts are correct: Article 49A(2)(c) contains an expulsion carve-out, Zuraida was decided in contract law, Suhaili did not produce a merits ruling on Article 49A(2)(c), and the Rafizi-Nik Nazmi vacancies were officially treated under Article 51. However, the analysis has material publication-risk defects: the Act number is wrong, 'four years' is an overclaim, Suhaili is framed imprecisely, the Zuraida constitutional-right characterisation is incorrect, and the 'zero substantive judicial interpretation' claim is not safely verified as an absolute absence claim. Recommended status: needs revision before publication.
